title: MCP Tools
concept: Callable functions a server exposes that the model can invoke, with typed inputs and outputs, mirroring function calling.

exercises.prompt: Without protocols like MCP, what is a major limitation of standard Large Language Models?

options.text: They cannot interact with external files, APIs, or local environments on their own.
They cannot generate grammatically correct sentences.
They are unable to understand synonyms or basic human languages.

exercises.explain: As this is the first lesson of the unit on Tools, Resources, and Prompts, this warmup hooks the learner by introducing the core limitation of isolated LLMs.

cards.title: Giving LLMs Hands
cards.body: Large Language Models are great at talking, but they can't natively search your computer files, run terminal commands, or send emails. MCP Tools bridge this gap by giving LLMs a set of hands to interact with external systems.

cards.title: What are MCP Tools?
cards.body: MCP Tools are callable functions exposed by a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server. They let an AI model take action in the real world, like running code, searching the web, or editing files, using strict input and output schemas.

cards.title: The Office Speed-Dial
cards.body: Imagine your LLM is a manager sitting at a desk. An MCP Tool is like a specific desk phone speed-dial button. When pressed, it calls a specialist who performs a precise task and reads the result back, rather than the manager doing it themselves.

cards.title: A Real-World Call
cards.body: A weather MCP server might expose a tool named 'get_temperature' that requires a 'zip_code' string as input. When the user asks 'Is it cold in 90210?', the LLM calls this tool, receives '72°F', and answers the user.

cards.title: Who Runs the Code?
cards.body: Some think LLMs run tool code directly inside their own neural networks. In reality, the LLM only decides *which* tool to call and with what arguments; the external MCP server executes the actual code safely.

cards.title: Resource vs. Tool
cards.body: Comparing how an LLM retrieves static information versus how it takes dynamic action using tools.

left.text: Reading a text file or database record to gain context.

right.text: Executing a function that modifies data or runs a script.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary purpose of an MCP Tool?

options.text: They are read-only documents containing static background information.
They are callable functions with typed inputs that allow the model to take actions.
They are prompt templates used to format the user's initial message.
They are security protocols that encrypt user chat history.

exercises.explain: MCP Tools are designed to act as callable functions that let the model perform external actions, mirroring traditional LLM function calling.

exercises.prompt: True or False: MCP tools define clear, typed input parameters so the LLM knows exactly how to invoke them.
exercises.explain: MCP tools use strict typing (like JSON Schema) so the LLM knows exactly what parameters (like integers, booleans, or strings) it must provide.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing phrase to describe how MCP tools enforce structured communication.
exercises.sentence: MCP tools mirror function calling by using typed inputs and outputs to prevent errors.
exercises.explain: MCP Tools mirror standard function calling by requiring typed inputs and outputs, ensuring the LLM passes the correct parameter types (like strings or numbers).

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of an MCP Tool?

options.text: A read-only file list containing system logs for the LLM to inspect.
A server exposing a 'calculate_tax' tool with a JSON schema defining 'income' as a required number.
A pre-written prompt template that asks the LLM to write a creative story.

exercises.explain: Option B shows a system defining a tool with a name, description, and input schema, which is the standard way MCP servers expose tools to an AI model. Options A and C describe prompts and static resources.

exercises.prompt: As an application engineer, how would you best describe the architecture and function of MCP Tools to a developer joining your team?

options.text: They are server-defined callable functions with schema-defined typed inputs and outputs.
They are visual UI buttons that the user clicks to prompt the LLM manually.
They are hardcoded text templates stored in the client-side system instructions.
They are external databases that the LLM queries using standard SQL commands.

exercises.explain: MCP Tools are server-defined callable functions. They act exactly like standard LLM function calling, exposing typed inputs (parameters) and outputs so the model can reliably request actions like writing a file or query a database.
